source.title: Laboratory Evolution of Fast-Folding Green Fluorescent Protein Using Secretory Pathway Quality Control

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: GFP folding inefficiency in E. coli; existing variants (frGFP, sfGFP) show limited fluorescence difference in cytoplasm, hindering directed evolution. Hypothesis: SecYEG secretory pathway quality control can discriminate folding, enabling selection of superfolded variants.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Superfolder GFP (sfGFP) escapes SecYEG-mediated elimination, unlike other well-folded GFPs. This quality control was used to engineer superfolded GFPmut2 variants with faster folding kinetics, including superfast GFP, without loss of function.
process_signals: sfGFP spared from SecYEG elimination
Hypothesis: SecYEG quality control can be used for engineering superfolded proteins
Combinatorial library of GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG
Isolated variants with enhanced folding, faster kinetics, no loss in fluorescence
methods: Targeting GFP variants to SecYEG translocase

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: GFP folds inefficiently in E. coli. Existing superfolder and supercharged GFPs show improved properties. The lag in engineering such variants suggests screening limitations. The study aims to use SecYEG quality control to evolve superfolded proteins.
process_signals: GFP folding inefficiency problem
Existing superproteins (sfGFP, supercharged GFP) show improved folding
Lag in engineering suggests screening limitations
Hypothesis: cellular quality control can exert evolutionary pressure to improve folding
SecYEG quality control discriminates folded states
failures_or_limits: Screening methods may be limiting progress

section_gists.heading: Exploring the use of cellular folding quality control to identify superfolded proteins
section_gists.gist: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only 65% fluorescence increase, insufficient for FACS. SecYEG targeting yielded 24-67 fold difference due to cytoplasmic accumulation of sfGFP and degradation of frGFP. Tat pathway gave modest difference.
process_signals: Initial goal: develop cellular screen to differentiate frGFP and sfGFP
Cytoplasmic screen insufficient (only 65% increase)
Tat pathway gave modest difference
SecYEG targeting gave 24-67 fold fluorescence difference
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm when targeted to SecYEG; frGFP degraded
Differential cytoplasmic stability enables discrimination
methods: Targeting to Tat and SecYEG pathways
failures_or_limits: Tat pathway gave only modest difference

section_gists.heading: Exploiting SecYEG-mediated quality control to engineer folding-enhanced proteins
section_gists.gist: GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG showed low fluorescence. A combinatorial library of GFPmut2 was created by error-prone PCR and screened via FACS. Nine variants (P1-P9) with increased fluorescence were isolated, containing recurring substitutions. TrxA fusion also increased fluorescence.
process_signals: GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG is 46-fold less fluorescent than sfGFP
First approach: TrxA fusion increased fluorescence, confirming folding trap hypothesis
Library construction: error-prone PCR of GFPmut2, fused to ssDsbA
FACS screening: two rounds, sorting top fluorescent cells
Nine variants (P1-P9) isolated with increased fluorescence
Recurring substitutions identified: N105D, A206V, etc.
Variants accumulate in cytoplasm
failures_or_limits: Initial GFPmut2-SecYEG fluorescence too low for direct screening

section_gists.heading: Characterization of the folding-enhanced GFPmut2 variants
section_gists.gist: Variants P4, P5, P7 showed increased fluorescence and cytoplasmic accumulation. Without signal peptide, they retained fluorescence. Refolding kinetics: P4, P5 comparable to sfGFP; P7 (superfast GFP) faster. All showed increased stability to GdnHCl. Soluble yield slightly lower.
process_signals: Re-transformation confirmed increased fluorescence
Without signal peptide, variants retain fluorescence activity
P4, P5, P7 chosen for detailed characterization
Refolding: P4, P5 comparable to sfGFP; P7 faster (superfast GFP)
All variants show increased stability to chemical denaturation
Soluble yield slightly lower than GFPmut2
Mutations N105D, A206V do not affect folding kinetics
failures_or_limits: frGFP showed modest stability, not expected

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: SecYEG quality control enables evolution of superfolded proteins. Cytoplasmic accumulation depends on kinetic competition between folding and degradation. Overflow due to SRP saturation may contribute. Screening methods, not inherent difficulty, limited previous engineering. Single round of mutagenesis and two FACS rounds yielded superfolded variants.
process_signals: SecYEG quality control successfully used for evolution
Cytoplasmic accumulation linked to folding kinetics and SRP saturation
Overflow substrates may accumulate if they fold fast
Need to optimize promoter/induction to avoid saturating quality control
Screening methods were limiting, not inherent difficulty
Single mutagenesis round and two FACS rounds sufficient
failures_or_limits: Overflow may saturate quality control; need optimization

section_gists.heading: Materials and Methods
section_gists.gist: Materials and Methods section header, no content.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial cytoplasmic screen insufficient → shift to SecYEG quality control
SecYEG quality control discriminates folding by retaining fast-folders in cytoplasm
GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG provides low baseline for selection
Error-prone PCR library and FACS can isolate superfolded variants
Folding kinetics, not stability or yield, drives cytoplasmic accumulation
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact library size and screening details not fully described in these sections
Mechanism of cytoplasmic accumulation (SRP saturation vs. folding kinetics) not fully resolved
Generalizability to other proteins beyond GFP and TrxA
Role of specific proteases in degradation of non-superfolded variants

events.summary: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only a small fluorescence increase, insufficient for FACS-based directed evolution.
Hypothesis that SecYEG-mediated quality control can discriminate folding and be used to engineer superfolded proteins.
Targeted sfGFP and frGFP to SecYEG via different signal peptides and observed large fluorescence differences.
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm when targeted to SecYEG, while frGFP is degraded, enabling discrimination.
GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG via ssDsbA was 46-fold less fluorescent than sfGFP, providing a low baseline for selection.
Fusion of TrxA to GFPmut2 increased fluorescence, confirming that rapid folding can trap protein in cytoplasm.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction and early results, establishing the problem, hypothesis, and initial experiments with SecYEG targeting.

events.summary: Performed two rounds of FACS screening and isolated 9 colonies with fluorescent phenotypes above the continuum distribution.
Sequenced variants and identified 7 recurring substitutions, including N105Y, F64L, E124V, K101E, A206V, G232D, V163A.
Re-transformed plasmids P1-P9 into fresh cells and confirmed increased fluorescence and cytoplasmic accumulation.
Subcloned variants without signal peptide and observed little loss in fluorescence, indicating folding improvement without functional loss.
Characterized P4, P5, P7: slightly lower soluble yield and fluorescence, but refolding kinetics comparable to or faster than sfGFP; P7 renamed superfast GFP.
Kinetic unfolding revealed superfast GFP and P5 unfold slowly, P4 unfolds like GFPmut2; variants isolated primarily on improved folding kinetics.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers library construction, FACS screening, variant characterization, and folding analysis.

events.summary: Hypothesis that cytoplasmic accumulation of SRP substrates is linked to both SRP saturation and quality control, with a kinetic competition between folding and degradation.
Analysis that screening methods, not inherent difficulty, limited engineering of folding-enhanced GFP variants.
Design of library construction: error-prone PCR of GFPmut2 fused to DsbA signal peptide.
methods_or_tools: mutagenic method of Fromant et al.

events.summary: Limitation: need to optimize promoter/induction conditions to avoid saturating quality control machinery.
Validation: single round of mutagenesis and two FACS rounds yielded superfolded clones P4, P5, and superfast GFP.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion of mechanism, experimental details for library screening and protein folding analysis, and validation of the approach.

evidence_bank.quote: there was only a small increase (65%) in the geometric mean fluorescence of cells expressing sfGFP relative to frGFP

we explored the hypothesis that native cellular quality control pathways exert sufficient evolutionary pressure to improve protein folding properties beyond those that are normally sufficient for solubility and function within a cell.
we observed a 67-, 47- and 24-fold greater fluorescence emission for cells expressing ssDsbA-sfGFP, ssMBP-sfGFP and ssPhoA-sfGFP fusions, respectively, relative to the corresponding frGFP fusions
subcellular fractionation revealed that the fluorescence emitted by cells expressing the sfGFP fusions was localized predominantly in the cytoplasmic fraction
ssDsbA-frGFP is efficiently degraded and does not accumulate anywhere in cells
cells expressing GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG via ssDsbA were 46-fold less fluorescent than cells expressing ssDsbA-sfGFP
Our first approach was via direct fusion of GFPmut2 to thioredoxin-1 (TrxA), an extremely well-folded protein known to fold too rapidly for Sec
Following two rounds of FACS we isolated 9 colonies that displayed fluorescent phenotypes significantly above the continuum distribution.

Of the 35 total substitutions that were found, 7 specific substitutions occurred more than once and accounted for 19 of the 35 total substitutions. These recurring substitutions with frequencies in parenthesis were: N105Y (4×), F64L (4×), E124V (3×), K101E (2×), A206V (2×), G232D (2×) and V163A (2×).

we first re-transformed the recovered plasmids (clones P1–P9) into fresh cells and observed that re-transformed cells expressing variants P1–P9 all showed increased fluorescence relative to ssDsbA-GFPmut2 (Fig. 2a) as a result of cytoplasmic accumulation of each variant (Fig. 1d, e).

We subcloned the variants appended with a C-terminal 6x-histidine tag but without the coding region for ssDsbA and observed that cells expressing these signal sequence-less GFPmut2 variants showed little to no loss in fluorescence activity when compared to the parental GFPmut2 protein (Fig. 2b), indicating that any improvement in GFP folding (see below) was accomplished without significantly affecting the proteins' normal function.
Following purification, we observed that P4, P5, and P7 had a slightly lower soluble yield and slightly lower total fluorescence relative to GFPmut2 (Table 1)

the refolding speed of variants P4 and P5 was comparable to, while P7 far eclipsed, that of sfGFP (Fig. 3a, Table 1). Since sfGFP was one of the fastest folding GFPs to date [7], we renamed P7 'superfast' GFP.

we examined the kinetics of unfolding and discovered that superfast GFP and P5 unfolded very slowly whereas P4 unfolded at a speed comparable to GFPmut2 (Fig. 3c, Table 1). Thus, while we conclude that each of these variants appears to have been isolated primarily based on improved folding kinetics, laboratory evolution returned three unique responses to the pressure imposed by secretory quality control

there likely exists a kinetic competition between folding and degradation of overflow SRP substrates, suggesting that the cytoplasmic accumulation of an SRP-dependent protein is linked to both SRP saturation and quality control.
Based on the relative ease with which we engineered folding-enhanced variants of GFPmut2, the answer appears to be a lack of robust screening methods.
Libraries of GFPmut2 were synthesized using the mutagenic method of Fromant et al. [45] and inserted in-frame with the coding region for the DsbA signal peptide.

care needs to be taken to avoid further saturating the quality control machinery. The ability to broadly exploit this quality control feature in the future will likely depend on optimizing promoter/induction conditions.
a single round of mutagenesis and two rounds of FACS yielded superfolded clones P4, P5, and superfast GFP that each exhibited significant folding-enhancement relative to the parental GFPmut2 protein.

In just these 9 clones, we isolated mutations that were uncovered in each previous folding-enhanced variant of GFP: V163A is a cycle 3 mutation (we also found M153K) [2], F64L is an enhanced GFP mutation [15], A206V and Y145F are superfolder mutations [7] and our most common mutation, N105Y, is found at the same residue as the N105T superfolder mutation.
These recurring substitutions with frequencies in parenthesis were: N105Y (4×), F64L (4×), E124V (3×), K101E (2×), A206V (2×), G232D (2×) and V163A (2×).
Some of the substitutions even appeared in combination multiple times, for example N105Y/E124V (3×) and even K101E/N105Y/E124V/G232D (2×).
introduced random mutations to only 20 amino acids flanking the chromophore of wildtype GFP and successfully isolated a well-folded, FACS-optimized GFP variant (S65A/V68L/S72A) [4].
In addition, all variants carry the GFPmut2 substitutions (S65A/V68L/S72A).

This notable lag led us and others [13] to question whether this was due to inherent difficulties in engineering folding-enhanced variants of GFP or whether methods of screening and selecting were limiting progress.

To address this problem, several folding-enhanced variants of GFP have been created over the years that have effectively elevated GFP to one of the most studied and exploited proteins in biochemistry and cell biology $[2,4]$ .

Following purification, we observed that P4, P5, and P7 had a slightly lower soluble yield and slightly lower total fluorescence relative to GFPmut2 (Table 1), suggesting that these clones represent a novel solution to protein folding optimization as neither the function nor the soluble yield of the protein-of-interest was improved.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: GFP folding inefficiency in E. coli; existing variants (frGFP, sfGFP) show limited fluorescence difference in cytoplasm, hindering directed evolution. Hypothesis: SecYEG secretory pathway quality control can discriminate folding, enabling selection of superfolded variants.

canonical_events.summary: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only a 65% increase in geometric mean fluorescence, insufficient for FACS-based directed evolution.
Hypothesis that SecYEG-mediated quality control can discriminate folding and be used to engineer superfolded proteins.
Targeted sfGFP and frGFP to SecYEG via different signal peptides (ssDsbA, ssMBP, ssPhoA) and observed large fluorescence differences (24-67 fold).
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm when targeted to SecYEG, while frGFP is degraded, enabling discrimination based on folding.
GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG via ssDsbA was 46-fold less fluorescent than sfGFP, providing a low baseline for selection.
Fusion of TrxA to GFPmut2 increased fluorescence, confirming that rapid folding can trap protein in cytoplasm.
Design of library construction: error-prone PCR of GFPmut2 fused to DsbA signal peptide.
Performed two rounds of FACS screening and isolated 9 colonies (P1-P9) with fluorescent phenotypes above the continuum distribution.
Sequenced variants and identified 7 recurring substitutions: N105Y, F64L, E124V, K101E, A206V, G232D, V163A.
Re-transformed plasmids P1-P9 into fresh cells and confirmed increased fluorescence and cytoplasmic accumulation.
Subcloned variants without signal peptide and observed little loss in fluorescence, indicating folding improvement without functional loss.
Characterized P4, P5, P7: slightly lower soluble yield and fluorescence, but refolding kinetics comparable to or faster than sfGFP; P7 renamed superfast GFP.
Kinetic unfolding revealed superfast GFP and P5 unfold slowly, P4 unfolds like GFPmut2; variants isolated primarily on improved folding kinetics.
Limitation: need to optimize promoter/induction conditions to avoid saturating quality control machinery.
Validation: single round of mutagenesis and two FACS rounds yielded superfolded clones P4, P5, and superfast GFP.

edges.reason: The problem of insufficient cytoplasmic fluorescence difference motivates the hypothesis to use SecYEG quality control.
The hypothesis leads to testing SecYEG targeting with different signal peptides.
The large fluorescence differences observed lead to analysis of subcellular localization.
Understanding that cytoplasmic accumulation enables discrimination leads to testing GFPmut2 as a baseline.
The low baseline of GFPmut2 prompts testing TrxA fusion to confirm folding trap hypothesis.
The low baseline of GFPmut2 motivates library design for evolution.
Isolation of colonies leads to sequencing and identification of substitutions.
Isolated colonies are re-transformed for validation.
Validation of fluorescence leads to subcloning without signal peptide to confirm folding improvement.
Confirmation of folding improvement leads to detailed characterization of selected variants.
Refolding kinetics analysis leads to kinetic unfolding studies to understand mechanism.
Understanding the mechanism highlights the limitation of saturating quality control.
The successful isolation of superfolded variants validates the overall approach.

must_cover_points: SecYEG targeting yields large fluorescence difference
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm, frGFP degraded
Variants retain fluorescence without signal peptide
P4, P5, P7 characterized; P7 is superfast GFP
Variants isolated on improved folding kinetics
Single round mutagenesis and two FACS rounds sufficient
